to adulthood ?

to adulthood ?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and sustained focus on the research question throughout the essay
Effective communication of topic via coherent section headings aligned with the investigationâs purpose
Well-formulated and focused research question consistently linked back to each discussion point
weaknesses: Occasional digression into general definitions of depression without direct linkage to transition outcomes
Lack of explicit description of the overall search strategy and inclusion/exclusion criteria in methodology
Insufficient detail on participant recruitment and potential sampling biases
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place topic communication and question formulation securely in the 5â6 band, with only brief off-topic definitions preventing the very top mark. Methodology is mostly complete but lacks a dedicated overview of search procedures and recruitment details, aligning with the 3â4 band at its upper end. Balancing these, a score of 5/6 accurately reflects strong clarity and focus with room for more comprehensive methodological exposition.

strengths: Good command of core psychological theories and empirical findings (e.g. Verdeli, Silberg)
Accurate use of most subject-specific terminology (emotion regulation, HPA-axis, intergenerational transmission)
Diverse bibliography demonstrating engagement with longitudinal studies and systematic reviews
weaknesses: Minor factual inaccuracies (e.g. source attributions conflating WHO and Harvard Medical School)
Occasional reliance on popular-press websites undermining academic rigor
Inconsistent acronym formatting and occasional grammatical lapses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding is solidly in the 3â4 band: the student shows good subject knowledge and mostly accurate terminology use. However, sporadic inaccuracies and inconsistent presentation of technical terms prevent full marks, making 4/6 the appropriate score.

strengths: Selection of longitudinal and systematic studies demonstrates discerning source choice relevant to the research question
Clear analytical comments on sample bias, historical context, and study limitations
Reasoned structure linking evidence on depressionâs effects to transition outcomes
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on descriptive summaries limits depth of independent interpretation
Conclusions occasionally asserted without full justification
Lack of evaluation of modern contextual factors (e.g. social media) reduces critical breadth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Across the three Critical Thinking strands, research quality aligns with the 7â9 band, while analysis depth and evaluation fit the 4â6 band. The combined performanceâgood relevance and some supported analysis but uneven critical evaluationâplaces the criterion at the lower end of the 7â9 band. A holistic score of 7/12 reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Logical overall structure with a clear title page, contents, introduction, main sections, conclusion, and bibliography
Table of contents clearly ordered to guide the reader
weaknesses: Missing abstract or summary to frame the investigation upfront
Inconsistent in-text citation style and occasional split URLs
Manual pagination and uneven formatting of headings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets most structural conventions and has a clear layout, but missing an abstract, inconsistent referencing, and manual page numbering prevent full marks. A mid-range score of 3/4 accurately captures generally appropriate structure with formatting gaps.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Psychological terminology is used mostly correctly and in context.** Terms such as âemotion regulationâ, âintergenerational transmissionâ, âHPA axisâ, and statistical indicators like âWALDâ are employed accurately.  

Nonetheless, minor lapses (e.g., âcuredâ instead of âtreatedâ, inconsistent acronym formatting) and occasional grammatical errors detract from full consistency, keeping the strand in the middle band but justifying the higher score of 4.

**Basic layout conventions are addressed but inconsistently.** A word count, title, and table of contents are provided, and most references appear in a list. Yet in-text citations are incomplete, referencing style is inconsistent, and some URLs are split or lack retrieval dates.  

Formatting of tables and italics is uneven, and page numbers in the submission extract suggest manual rather than automatic pagination. These shortcomings leave the work in the 1-2 band, with a score of 2 for partial compliance.

**The student demonstrates good command of core psychological theories and findings.** Accurate summaries of longitudinal research (e.g., Verdeli et al., Silberg et al.) and concepts such as emotion regulation, HPA-axis functioning, and resilience show substantive knowledge. Sources are generally applied to support points, and limitations of key studies are acknowledged.  

However, occasional inaccuracies (confusing Harvard Medical School with WHO; conflating types of depression) and reliance on popular-press websites in places weaken consistency. The work therefore fits securely in the 3-4 band, at the upper end.

**A mostly appropriate range of secondary sources is used, but the methodological approach is only partially articulated.** About twenty references include longitudinal studies, systematic reviews and meta-analytic papers, showing informed selection beyond textbooks. The student also comments briefly on sampling, ethics and generalisability for several key studies, indicating some methodological awareness.  

Nevertheless, there is no explicit description of the overall research strategy (e.g., criteria for inclusion, search process), primary data are not gathered, and citation style is inconsistent. These gaps mean the methodology is âmostly completeâ rather than âcomprehensiveâ, placing it near the top of the 3-4 band.

**The topic is explicit and sustained throughout the essay.** The student states the focus (âHow does parental major depressive disorder impact teenagersâ transition to adulthood?â) on the title page and reiterates it in the introduction, signalling a clear purpose. Subsequent section headings (âHow Parental MDD Affects Transition to Adult Lifeâ, âImpact â¦ on Anxiety and Self-Regulationâ) align closely with this focus and are referenced in the conclusion, showing the line of inquiry has been kept in view.  

Minor weaknesses appear in occasional digressions on general definitions of depression that do not directly advance the investigation of transition; however, these are brief and do not obscure the central aim. Overall, clarity and appropriateness of topic communication warrant a mark in the upper band but not the very top.

**Most of the cited research is relevant and of reasonable quality.** The student selects longitudinal and systematic studies that squarely address parental depression and developmental outcomes, demonstrating discernment beyond anecdotal evidence.  

Some sources are secondary websites or newspaper-style summaries, preventing the submission from reaching the very top of the band, but the overall selection demonstrates âgoodâ rather than merely âadequateâ relevance.

**The research question is clearly worded, focused, and consistently referenced.** It specifies the target population (teenagers), the independent variable (parental MDD), and the outcome (transition to adulthood). Throughout the body the student repeatedly links each study back to this question, demonstrating connection to the discussion.  

Focus could be tightened further by defining âtransition to adulthoodâ operationally (e.g., educational, occupational, relational outcomes), but the existing wording is sufficiently narrow for an EE and avoids over-breadth. Hence the work fits solidly into the 5â6 band, meriting 5.

**The report follows standard EE conventions** (title page, contents, introduction, main sections, conclusion, bibliography). Headings and page progression are logical, and the argument is traceable.  

Nevertheless, some expected elements (abstract, consistent in-text citation style) are missing or weak, and pagination in the extract is irregular. These issues prevent full compliance but still reflect a generally appropriate structure, justifying the lower value within the top band.

**A reasoned line of argument is evident but only partially critical.** The structure moves from depressionâs direct effects to anxiety and emotion regulation, culminating in a conclusion that integrates key findings. The student does evaluate each studyâs strengths and weaknesses, yet the evaluation rarely feeds into weighing competing explanations or alternative perspectives.  

Because critical depth and synthesis are present but not sustained, the strand fits a solid âpartially criticalâ description, meriting 4.

**The essay contains clear analytical comments and some evaluation of study limitations,** for example discussing sample bias and time-period effects in Myers et al. and Verdeli et al. The student attempts to synthesise findings (e.g., linking emotion-regulation deficits to anxiety in transition).  

However, much space is devoted to descriptive summaries; independent interpretation is moderate and causal claims are sometimes asserted without full justification. Support for conclusions is therefore uneven, positioning the work at the lower end of the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The title page clearly states both the investigation title and the research question, demonstrating strong focus and alignment with the investigationâs purpose.
The absence of an abstract or summary reduces clarity for the reader; include a concise abstract outlining scope, methods, and key findings.
The table of contents is clear and logically ordered, helping readers navigate the structure effectively.
The critical evaluation of outdated data (1989â1996) reflects strong analytical awareness of historical context limitations.
The conclusion synthesizes major findings on intergenerational transmission and resilience, providing coherent closure.
Key studies on emotion regulation are omitted from the conclusion; include a brief mention of De Raeymaecker & Dhar to complete synthesis.
Bibliography contains a diverse range of scholarly and web sources, demonstrating comprehensive research.
Reference entries mix APA, Harvard, and direct URLs without consistency; adopt and apply a single citation style uniformly.
Entries in the contents table lack consistent indentation and formatting; adjust cell alignment to match IB conventions.
Opening definition of depression diverges into general statistics without linking to transition to adulthood; refocus introduction on parental MDD impacts.
Several source attributions conflate WHO and Harvard Medical School; verify each sourceâs origin to maintain credibility.
The introduction integrates key developmental theories (Arnett, Erikson, Marcia) to frame identity formation; demonstrates sound conceptual understanding.
Some claims lack in-text citations (e.g., Gerstacker, 2010); ensure every fact and quotation is properly referenced.
Section header contains a spelling error: âeffectsâ should read âaffectsâ in the context of impacts; correct to maintain professionalism.
Methodological approach lacks explicit description of search strategy, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and database selection; add a dedicated methodology subsection.
Presentation of key statistics from Myers et al. is clear and supports the argument about transition stressors.
Description of the participant group omits clear explanation of recruitment and sampling bias; clarify recruitment criteria and potential limitations.
No discussion on how modern factors (social media, digital communication) might alter applicability; include contemporary context evaluation.
The discussion of WALD statistics demonstrates accurate use of specialized statistical terminology.
The explanation of WALD could be expanded for readers unfamiliar with this testâconsider a brief footnote on its interpretation.
Header âHow parental MDD impact anxiety and self regulation in transition to adulthoodâ lacks parallel structure with earlier headings; harmonize phrasing.
Repetition of Arnett (2001) content in multiple sections dilutes focus; integrate references more selectively.
The 20-year longitudinal design of the Verdeli et al. study is effectively highlighted, indicating careful source selection.
Participant numbers (e.g., 151 children) are mentioned but lack clarity on attrition rates; report dropout statistics to strengthen validity.
Methodological rigor in the systematic review is clear, showing careful database selection and inclusion of fifty papers.
The review critique fails to address long-term follow-up of emotional regulation outcomes; consider suggesting longitudinal studies.
Several URLs in references are split across lines and lack access dates; ensure retrieval details and continuous formatting.
Consistent application of hanging indents in the bibliography would enhance readability and professional presentation.
Page numbering appears manually placed; ensure footer auto-numbering to avoid layout inconsistencies.
The presence of the required word count at the start of the document fulfills formatting guidelines.
The title block and subject declaration satisfy the formatting requirement for an EE title page.
The report does not exceed the 4,000-word limit, as indicated by the word count, ensuring compliance.
